nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Lake Turkana (formerly Lake Rudolf), the world’s largest permanent alkaline desert lake. Shallow rocky and sandy shoreline zones with volcanic substrate; benthopelagic. The lake has no surface drainage outlet, producing highly alkaline and slightly saline conditions (natural pH 9.1–9.4). Aquacultured fish adapt readily to more moderate aquarium chemistry.

decorPreferences: flat rocks or slate for spawning sites
clay pots or flowerpots turned on their sides
pebble and rock stacks to break lines of sight
epiphyte plants (Java fern, Anubias, Java moss) attached to hardscape — fish will uproot rooted plants when digging

diet.notes: Accepts most quality prepared cichlid foods — sinking pellets and granules are preferred over surface flakes for these mid-to-bottom dwellers. Supplement with frozen and live items: spirulina-enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, bloodworm, mosquito larvae, and daphnia. Spirulina-rich foods help maintain the species’ brilliant red coloration. Blanched vegetables such as spinach are also accepted. Avoid over-reliance on dry foods alone.

goodWith: larger Congo tetras and similar robust open-water tetras
non-cichlid mid-water fish of similar size
other cichlids only in large tanks with ample cover
avoidWith: small fish that may be eaten (rasboras, small tetras, nano fish)
highly aggressive or larger cichlids that will bully the pair
any tankmates when an established pair is actively spawning in smaller aquaria

summary: The Turkana Jewel Cichlid (Rubricatochromis exsul) is a small, vividly colored East African cichlid endemic to Kenya’s Lake Turkana. One of the smaller jewel cichlids, it reaches about 10 cm and is prized for its deep red-orange body, iridescent blue jewel spots, and distinctively dark fins.
careNotes:
Rubricatochromis exsul is an endemic of Lake Turkana, Kenya — a vast alkaline rift lake with no outlet and naturally extreme water chemistry (pH 9.1–9.4). Aquacultured specimens readily adapt to moderate aquarium conditions (pH 7.5–8.5, gH 10–20, kH 8–15, 24–27°C), though keeping water on the hard, alkaline side is generally associated with better coloration and reduced disease susceptibility. Soft, acidic water should be avoided.

A 110–150 L aquarium with a minimum 90 cm footprint is appropriate for a single pair; a 150+ L tank with 120 cm footprint is strongly recommended if tankmates are desired, as the pair’s territorial aggression tends to extend across the full tank during spawning. Provide dense cover: flat rocks or slate for spawning, clay pots as retreats, and rock stacks to break lines of sight. Epiphyte plants (Java fern, Anubias, Java moss) attached to hardscape fare better than rooted plants because this species digs vigorously during breeding preparations.

Outside of breeding, R. exsul tends to be moderately active and tolerant toward similarly sized robust fish. Once pair bonds form and spawning commences, the fish can become highly aggressive toward all tankmates. In smaller setups it is often most practical to keep the pair alone. This species forms strong monogamous pair bonds and established pairs should not be split.

Water quality should be maintained with moderate to strong filtration and weekly partial water changes of 20–25%. The species is generally considered very hardy but will show color fade and increased disease susceptibility in poor conditions or soft acidic water. Unlike its larger relative Rubricatochromis bimaculatus (the common jewel cichlid), R. exsul’s smaller adult size of 7–10 cm makes it somewhat more manageable in a community setting.

Taxonomic note: this species was formerly classified as Hemichromis exsul before being moved into the new genus Rubricatochromis in 2022, which separated the red jewel cichlids from the five-spotted cichlids that remain in Hemichromis. The common name “Dwarf Jewel Cichlid” references its smaller size compared to other jewels; “Turkana Jewel Cichlid” is the most precise hobbyist name.
breedingNotes:
Rubricatochromis exsul forms strong monogamous pair bonds and spawns readily in captivity, often breeding year-round once a pair matures at 4–5 cm. It is a biparental substrate spawner: the male selects and thoroughly cleans a flat rock, slate slab, or smooth hard surface, and the female deposits a clutch of several hundred adhesive eggs directly onto it. Both parents take an active role guarding the spawning site and later the free-swimming fry with considerable aggression toward all other fish in the aquarium.

To obtain a breeding pair, start with a group of 5–6 juveniles raised together and allow natural pairing to occur. Forced pairing of adults often results in aggression. Provide clay pots, flat rocks, and multiple retreat areas to reduce conflict.

Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at 26°C; wrigglers become free-swimming fry within a further 3–4 days. First foods for fry include freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, and finely powdered larval food. Parental care is intense and both fish guard the fry for several weeks. In a species-only tank or dedicated breeding setup, fry survival is high. During breeding, this species undergoes a dramatic intensification of body coloration — females in particular develop an almost luminous orange-red hue that is striking in the aquarium.

primary.name: Aquatics Unlimited — Hemichromis exsul (Jewel Turkana / Red Dwarf)
primary.notes: Primary trade care reference. Provided water parameters (75–79°F, pH 7.5–8.5, KH 10–15), adult size (3.5–4 inches), tank minimum (20 gal for juveniles), diet (prepared cichlid foods, frozen brine shrimp/mysis), temperament (semi-aggressive), and compatibility guidance. Lake Turkana origin and alkaline lake context confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Rubricatochromis exsul (summary 62496)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), accepted scientific name Rubricatochromis exsul, native range (Africa: Lake Turkana), maximum size 10.0 cm SL, freshwater benthopelagic tropical habitat. Adult size ceiling of 10 cm SL used as max in adultSizeCm.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Rubricatochromis exsul (= Hemichromis exsul)
additional.notes: Confirmed Lake Turkana (formerly Lake Rudolf), Kenya. Sexual maturity at 4–5 cm TL. Breeding year-round in captivity. Strikingly dark fins and absent lateral spot distinguish species. Bred by Lothar Seegers in 1998 after live collection. Reclassification to Rubricatochromis by Lamboj & Koblmüller 2022 (Hydrobiologia) confirmed.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — Turkana Jewel Cichlid (Rubricatochromis exsul)
additional.notes: Provided temperature (75–80°F / 24–27°C), pH 7.0–8.5, minimum 30 gal tank, diet (pellets + frozen meaty foods), adult size ~4 inches. Described as “one of the smaller and more docile members of its genus,” hardy and adaptable. Alkaline-tolerant note confirmed.

additional.name: fishkeeper.co.uk — Turkana Jewel Cichlid (Rubricatochromis exsul)
additional.notes: Confirmed native range as Kenya: Lake Turkana. Temperature 23–27°C, pH 7.0–8.0, KH 6–20. Max 7 cm in wild, may grow larger in captivity. Sandy substrate, flat rocks/slate for spawning, clay pots as retreats. Specialist community fish; parents guard fry ~4 weeks. Synonyms Pelmatochromis exsul and Hemichromis exsul listed.

additional.name: Quality Marine — The Red Jewel of Turkana
additional.notes: Described natural Lake Turkana chemistry (pH 9.1–9.4, 2500 ppm salinity); fish adapt to aquarium conditions at pH 7.0–8.0. Tank 40 gal minimum for breeding pair, 4+ ft for multiple pairs. Substrate spawner on flat rocks/clay pots; female lays hundreds of eggs; fry first foods brine shrimp nauplii. Dramatic breeding coloration change noted.

fish.breedingNotes: This species is a biparental substrate spawner that forms strong monogamous pair bonds. Provide flat rocks or slate as spawning sites and clay pots as retreats. To obtain a compatible pair, start with 5–6 juveniles and allow natural pairing to occur. The female deposits several hundred eggs on a cleaned flat surface, and both parents guard the eggs — which typically hatch in approximately 2–3 days — and the free-swimming fry that follow. Fry accept brine shrimp nauplii and microworms as first foods. Once sexually mature at around 4–5 cm, established pairs generally breed readily throughout the year.
